summary: Replaced an inline PowerPC assembly implementation of the local `fmod` helper in `src/sysdolphin/baselib/aobj.c` with a short matching C implementation attributed by the PR title to Cam. The function keeps the same observed behavior: if `fabs(b) > fabs(a)`, return `a`; otherwise compute an integer quotient from `a / b` and return `a - b * quotient`. This removed a block of hand-written assembly around `__cvt_dbl_usll` / `__cvt_sll_flt` calls and converted it into maintainable C while preserving the match.
what_changed: Removed the inline assembly body for `fmod` in `src/sysdolphin/baselib/aobj.c`.
Added a C implementation: `float fmod(float a, float b) { long long quotient; if (fabs(b) > fabs(a)) return a; quotient = a / b; return a - b * quotient; }`.

The replacement captures the assembly's control flow: compare absolute values first, early-return the dividend when the divisor magnitude is larger, otherwise truncate/convert the division result through a `long long` quotient before subtracting `b * quotient`.

The old assembly saved/restored floating registers and called conversion helpers `__cvt_dbl_usll` and `__cvt_sll_flt`; the new C relies on the compiler to reproduce the same quotient-conversion pattern.
smart_moves: Used a `long long quotient` temporary rather than a floating temporary, matching the assembly's integer-conversion sequence after `a / b`.
Preserved the early `fabs(b) > fabs(a)` branch, which avoids unnecessary division/conversion and matches the old branch structure.
Kept the implementation minimal and local, reducing a 28-line assembly block to a 6-line C function without changing surrounding AObj code.
The expression `a - b * quotient` directly corresponds to the observed final negative multiply-subtract behavior of the assembly, making the intent clearer while still serving matching.
decomp_lessons: For small math helpers, the key to replacing assembly may be reproducing conversion semantics, not just using the standard library operation with the same name.
When assembly calls compiler conversion helpers such as `__cvt_dbl_usll` and `__cvt_sll_flt`, try modeling the value with an explicit integer temporary of the corresponding width.
Magnitude guard branches around math operations can be essential for matching and should be carried over exactly before simplifying expressions.

A function named like a standard C library routine may be a project-local implementation with nonstandard signature or behavior; here the matched C returns `float` and uses `float` parameters in the new source.
naming_conventions: The decompiled helper is named `fmod`, matching the existing symbol name from the assembly block.
The replacement uses simple parameter names `a` and `b` and a semantic local `quotient`, making the arithmetic clearer than labels such as `lbl_80364374` and `lbl_80364384`.
No broader project naming guidance or reviewer-requested renames are present in this PR slice.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Translate `fabs` / `fcmpo` / branch patterns into an explicit `if (fabs(b) > fabs(a)) return a;`.
Model `fdivs` followed by integer conversion helper calls with `quotient = a / b;` where `quotient` is `long long`.
Use `return a - b * quotient;` to reproduce the modulo-like remainder calculation after quotient truncation.
This PR demonstrates an asm-to-C match where the correct C type of an intermediate controls generated conversion code.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that adjust or move the local `fmod` implementation.
Search for other inline assembly math helpers that call `__cvt_dbl_usll` or `__cvt_sll_flt`.
Check whether this local `fmod` conflicts with standard library `fmod` declarations in later headers or build configurations.
Look for additional Cam-attributed matches in sysdolphin/baselib.
